top_stories.headline: Iran War Ceasefire Holds, Hormuz Still Effectively Closed
top_stories.one_liner: A US-Iran 60-day ceasefire framework is in place and Hormuz de-mining is underway, but traffic at the strait remains at roughly 5% of pre-war levels, keeping global energy markets on edge.
top_stories.detail: The 2026 Iran war, initiated by US and Israeli strikes on February 28 that killed Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei, has settled into an uneasy ceasefire extended indefinitely by Trump in late April. Pakistan mediated a two-week truce on April 8 that has since been extended, but the Strait of Hormuz — through which around 20% of global petroleum and LNG normally flows — remains effectively closed, with vessel traffic at roughly 5% of pre-war levels. A 60-day framework reported by the Washington Post in late May includes de-mining the strait and a pathway to a final nuclear and security deal, but implementation remains incomplete. Hezbollah's rejection of the latest ceasefire agreement as of June 4 (AP) and continued Israeli strikes in Lebanon are a fresh complication, since Iran has previously paused Hormuz traffic in retaliation for Lebanese theater flare-ups. The House of Commons Library notes a 'dual blockade' persists: Iran restricts commercial access while the US maintains a counter-blockade on ships bound for Iranian ports. The Fed's April FOMC minutes flagged that developments in the Middle East are contributing to elevated inflation, with headline CPI up 3.8% year-on-year through April largely driven by energy costs. J.P. Morgan Research expects the Fed to hold rates steady for the rest of 2026, with the next move potentially a hike in Q3 2027, contingent on how the energy shock evolves. Watch: the pace of Hormuz de-mining, Lebanon ceasefire compliance, and whether the 60-day framework produces a verifiable nuclear deal framework before the extension lapses.

top_stories.headline: Anthropic Files Confidential IPO at $965B Valuation, SpaceX S-1 Also Live
top_stories.one_liner: Anthropic's confidential S-1 — backed by $65B Series H and $47B annualized revenue — and SpaceX's public filing targeting up to $2T mark the most consequential AI/tech IPO moment in history.
top_stories.detail: Anthropic filed a confidential S-1 with the SEC on June 1, 2026, following a $65 billion Series H round that lifted its valuation to approximately $965 billion — eclipsing OpenAI for the first time. Annualized revenue crossed $47 billion in May, up from roughly $9 billion at end-2025, driven primarily by its coding agents and enterprise Claude deployments. Fortune reports that Anthropic is expected to join SpaceX and OpenAI as the three potential trillion-dollar listings of 2026. SpaceX simultaneously filed a public S-1 targeting a valuation between $1.75 trillion and $2 trillion, aiming to raise up to $75 billion. Goldman Sachs, JPMorgan, and Morgan Stanley are reported to be under consideration for lead roles on the Anthropic and OpenAI listings. TechCrunch reported Friday that Anthropic President Daniela Amodei is publicly deflecting doubts about AI's ROI as the company faces pre-IPO scrutiny, including an unresolved legal dispute with the Pentagon over a supply-chain risk designation. The broader AI IPO wave is prompting concern from Fortune and others that dot-com-era investor guardrails are being dismantled just as retirement accounts may be pushed into near-trillion-dollar listings. For investors, the key test ahead is whether audited financials and capex disclosures can sustain the private-market comps when they meet public scrutiny.

top_stories.headline: Trump Signs Frontier AI Security EO: Voluntary 30-Day Government Preview Window
top_stories.one_liner: The White House's June 2 executive order asks — but cannot compel — AI labs to give NSA and CISA a 30-day look at the most powerful models before release, triggered by Anthropic's Mythos finding 10,000+ critical vulnerabilities in a month.
top_stories.detail: President Trump signed 'Promoting Advanced Artificial Intelligence Innovation and Security' on June 2, 2026, the most detailed federal AI policy action since he revoked Biden's AI safety order in January 2025. The order establishes a voluntary framework under which labs building frontier models can give the government up to 30 days of preview access before releasing to enterprise customers. NSA gets 60 days to build a classified benchmarking process to designate 'covered frontier models.' Critically, the order explicitly bars any mandatory licensing or preclearance requirement — a deliberate walk-back from an earlier, more aggressive draft that had reportedly been shelved over innovation concerns. The trigger is concrete: Anthropic's Claude Mythos Preview identified more than 10,000 high- or critical-severity vulnerabilities in a single month, including a wolfSSL flaw affecting roughly 5 billion devices. Palo Alto Networks has warned that three weeks of model-assisted analysis now matches a full year of manual penetration testing, and that the window before attackers access similar capability 'has accelerated significantly.' Industry reaction split predictably: the Business Software Alliance praised the voluntary approach; former CISA official Doc McConnell warned that 'classified benchmarking, nondisclosure requirements, and early access pilots will delay getting these models into the hands of the cyber defenders who can put them to use today.' Federal News Network and TechJournal covered the signing closely; Federal News Network noted the order also directs DOJ to prioritize criminal prosecution of AI-enabled cyberattacks. Watch: whether labs voluntarily opt in, and whether the NSA benchmarking process quietly becomes a de facto standard.

top_stories.headline: May Jobs Report Drops Today; AI Layoffs Hit Record as Labor Market Softens
top_stories.one_liner: BLS releases the May Employment Situation at 8:30 a.m. ET — the first payroll print after employers cited AI for a record 38,579 job-cut announcements in May, the highest monthly total since tracking began.
top_stories.detail: The Bureau of Labor Statistics releases the May 2026 Employment Situation this morning at 8:30 a.m. ET — a critical data point for Fed policy ahead of the June 16-17 FOMC meeting. April's print surprised to the upside with 115,000 nonfarm payrolls, nearly double the consensus of 62,000-65,000, holding the unemployment rate at 4.3%. ADP's May private payroll estimate came in at 122,000, slightly above the 120,000 consensus, suggesting continued but unspectacular hiring. The more alarming signal comes from Challenger, Gray & Christmas: employers cited AI for 38,579 job-cut announcements in May — the highest monthly total for AI since the firm began tracking the reason in 2023. Yahoo Finance's live coverage noted the question for investors is 'whether this remains a tech restructuring story — or starts bleeding into the broader white-collar labor market.' The Fed's May Beige Book reported 'employment showed little to no change' in 11 of 12 districts. J.P. Morgan Research sees the Fed holding rates steady at 3.5-3.75% through year-end, with upside inflation risk (energy-driven CPI at 3.8% YoY in April) and downside employment risk pulling in opposite directions. Key watch points in today's print: unemployment rate above 4.3%, labor force participation trend, and average hourly earnings — any hot wage reading pushes rate cuts further out.

top_stories.headline: AI Data Center Buildout Stalls: 30-50% of 2026 Capacity Delayed by Grid Crisis
top_stories.one_liner: Transformer lead times now exceeding 160 weeks and switchgear sold out through 2028 are blocking the $650B+ AI infrastructure spending wave, shifting competitive advantage to whoever secured power early.
top_stories.detail: America's AI data center boom is colliding with the hard physics of an aging power grid. Sightline Climate reports that 30% to 50% of AI data centers scheduled to open in 2026 will be delayed or cancelled, with only 5 GW under construction out of 16 GW announced. The Wall Street Journal reported that a large portion of planned 2027 data center capacity has not yet broken ground. The primary bottleneck is electrical infrastructure: substation transformer lead times now exceed 160 weeks according to the latest industry data — up from roughly 50 weeks in 2021 — and switchgear is sold out through 2028. PJM Interconnection data shows capacity market prices for 2026-2027 delivery surged to $329/MW, more than ten times the $29/MW from two years prior, with rapid data center growth as the primary cause. Grid connection queues in major US utility territories stretch 3-7 years, while data centers are typically planned and built in 2-3 years. Hyperscalers are spending over $650 billion on AI infrastructure in 2026, but as one report bluntly noted, 'money alone cannot solve the physics.' Responses include the 'Bring Your Own Power' model — Meta's Columbus, Ohio campus is bypassing the grid entirely with on-site natural gas — and Microsoft's multi-GW wind PPAs in Europe. A PowerLines analysis of 51 US investor-owned utilities published in April 2026 found planned capex of at least $1.4 trillion through 2030, a 21% jump from a year earlier, with more than 30 citing data centers as a top growth driver. Watch: FERC's grid enhancement rulemaking and whether Congress moves permitting reform legislation this session.

sectors.name: Frontier Tech / AI
sectors.summary: The AI vulnerability crisis that prompted Trump's June 2 EO continues to deepen: Anthropic's Mythos Preview found over 10,000 critical-severity vulnerabilities in a single month, including a wolfSSL flaw affecting roughly 5 billion devices. Palo Alto Networks warned that three weeks of model-assisted penetration testing now matches a year of manual work, and that the window before attackers access equivalent capability has accelerated significantly. Separately, GitHub Copilot switched to token-based billing on June 1, with power users reporting 10x-50x projected cost increases, and Microsoft unveiled Project Polaris — an in-house coding model replacing GPT-4 Turbo for all Copilot subscribers starting in August.

sectors.name: Applied Tech & Industry
sectors.summary: Anthropic's confidential IPO filing at a $965 billion valuation — with $47 billion annualized revenue — and SpaceX's public S-1 targeting up to $2 trillion collectively represent the largest tech IPO moment in history. Meta's Muse Spark model API remains delayed with no new launch date as of June 4. Cognition (Devin) closed a $1B+ Series D at a $26 billion valuation on May 27, reporting $492M in annualized revenue and 50% month-over-month growth.

sectors.name: US Policy & Power
sectors.summary: Trump's June 2 AI executive order is the most significant federal AI policy action since January 2025, establishing voluntary frontier model security frameworks involving NSA, CISA, Treasury, and NIST. The Colorado AI Act's June 30 effective date is approaching, with federal preemption pressure from the White House's March 2026 National Policy Framework still unresolved in Congress. The May jobs report releasing today is the key macro data point ahead of the June 16-17 FOMC meeting.

sectors.name: AI Regulation & Governance
sectors.summary: Trump's 'Promoting Advanced AI Innovation and Security' EO signed June 2 establishes a voluntary 30-day government preview window for frontier models, explicitly prohibiting any mandatory licensing regime. Industry broadly praised the voluntary approach; civil society critics warned classified benchmarking could slow defensive deployment. The Colorado AI Act's contested June 30 effective date remains the most immediate compliance deadline for US AI developers, with a working group draft that would reset it to January 1, 2027, still pending legislative action.

sectors.name: Geopolitics
sectors.summary: The 2026 Iran war ceasefire, extended indefinitely by Trump in late April, faces fresh strain as Hezbollah rejected the latest Lebanon ceasefire agreement on June 4 and Israel continues strikes. The Strait of Hormuz remains effectively closed at roughly 5% of pre-war traffic levels, with a US-Iran 60-day framework including de-mining and nuclear talks in progress but not yet implemented. China and Russia's continued Eurasian strategic convergence and Beijing's push to internationalize the renminbi — including digital yuan dominance in cross-border payment platforms — add to the structural geopolitical backdrop.

sectors.name: China
sectors.summary: China's demand for high-end semiconductor imports surged in Q1 2026 driven by rising AI investment, even as consumer retail sales growth remains sluggish at 1.7% YoY in March. Passenger car exports rose 60.6% year-on-year in Q1 as Beijing's 'anti-involution' subsidies drove excess supply into export markets. The ECB's June 2026 international role of the euro report confirmed that settlement activity on China's CIPS cross-border payment system surged around the outbreak of the Iran war, with some ships reportedly making payments in renminbi or crypto to transit the Strait of Hormuz in March-April 2026.

sectors.name: Markets
sectors.summary: The May jobs report drops this morning — the pivotal input for the June 16-17 FOMC decision. The Fed held rates at 3.5-3.75% at its April 28-29 meeting, with J.P. Morgan Research forecasting no cuts through 2026. S&P 500 and Nasdaq 100 are posting double-digit gains year-to-date, giving bankers confidence to push the AI IPO window open. Inflation remains elevated at 3.8% YoY in April, primarily energy-driven from the Hormuz closure, while average hourly earnings rose just 3.6% — leaving workers with negative real wage growth.

sectors.name: Digital Finance
sectors.summary: Bitcoin crashed to $61,300 before recovering to $62,500 with $3 billion in liquidations over two days, and is now roughly 45% below its October 2025 all-time high of $126,296. JPMorgan warned this week that time is running short for the Clarity Act crypto market structure bill, citing stablecoin yield disputes as a key sticking point. The CFTC recently cleared Coinbase to offer global crypto perpetual futures, a potential structural shift for US crypto trading. Crypto funds recorded $1.67 billion in weekly outflows, with three-week total exceeding $4 billion.

sectors.name: Energy
sectors.summary: The Strait of Hormuz closure — now at roughly 5% of pre-war traffic — remains the dominant energy story, with global oil and gas prices elevated and benefiting Russia. AI data center power demand is the second major energy theme: Sightline Climate estimates 30-50% of announced 2026 US data center capacity will be delayed due to transformer shortages (lead times now 160+ weeks) and switchgear sold out through 2028. PJM capacity market prices surged over tenfold year-over-year, and EPRI projects Virginia's data center electricity share could reach 41-59% of state supply by 2030.

sectors.name: Defense & Security
sectors.summary: The 2026 Iran war's economic cost to the US military reached nearly $29 billion as of May 12, with the Pentagon requesting a further $200 billion from Congress. The AI vulnerability crisis — Anthropic's Mythos finding tens of thousands of exploitable flaws — is now formally embedded in US defense and cybersecurity policy via the June 2 EO, which directs NSA to build a classified benchmarking process for frontier AI cyber capabilities. EU governments are racing to develop drone warfare capabilities in partnership with Ukraine, with the UK and France hosting conferences on reopening the Hormuz strait and establishing an international defensive shipping mission.

sectors.name: Labor & Workforce
sectors.summary: Employers cited AI for a record 38,579 job-cut announcements in May — the highest monthly total since Challenger, Gray & Christmas began tracking the reason in 2023 — raising urgent questions about whether AI displacement is moving beyond tech restructuring into broader white-collar markets. The May BLS jobs report releasing today is expected to show continued but soft hiring; ADP put May private payrolls at 122,000. Labor force participation has been softening toward historic lows, and wage growth for hourly workers (1.7% posted) is running well below April's 3.8% CPI print.

deep_dive.topic: China's Predictive Dissident AI and the Chip Export Reckoning
deep_dive.headline: The AI That Sees Dissent Coming: China's Surveillance Upgrade Collides With Washington's Chip Policy Reversal
deep_dive.standfirst: Leaked documents reveal a Chinese firm is building AI to flag future dissidents before they act — and the only known brake on the technology is a US chip export regime that the Trump administration has been systematically loosening.
deep_dive.body:
On June 1, the New York Times published the results of a Vanderbilt University investigation into roughly 100,000 leaked internal documents from Geedge Networks, a Chinese technology company that already sells a commercial version of the Great Firewall to authoritarian states. The documents reveal that Geedge, working with its government-backed research arm MESA Lab, has been developing an AI system capable of something surveillance technology has never before attempted at scale: identifying potential political dissidents before they have taken any action whatsoever.

The system, internally known as Cyber Narrator, intercepts encrypted metadata and builds behavioral profiles of citizens by fusing location data, telecommunications records, and social media activity. AI models then classify individuals and flag what the company's own documents describe as 'unacceptable political tendencies.' The Vanderbilt researchers found that internal meeting notes from February 2024 show researchers discussing how to 'identify intent' and 'achieve discovery of harmful information' — language that, in the Chinese Communist Party's governance vocabulary, is a euphemism for pre-empting dissent. Brett Goldstein, director of Vanderbilt's Wicked Problems Lab, captured the gravity in a single phrase: 'This is what happens when mass surveillance meets AI.'

This is not an abstract research project confined to a Chinese lab. Geedge has already exported operational surveillance infrastructure — earlier-generation products, not the predictive layer — to Myanmar, Ethiopia, Kazakhstan, and Pakistan. The deployment pipeline exists and is proven. What the leaked documents show is that the predictive upgrade is the next product in line. China's Public Security Bureaus are, separately, racing to integrate DeepSeek into other predictive policing applications, according to US officials and experts. The architecture of algorithmic pre-crime is being industrialized, not merely theorized.

The most politically explosive finding from the Vanderbilt report, however, is not what the technology can do — it is what has slowed it down. According to US officials cited in the investigation, Geedge's progress has been materially hampered by Biden-era chip export controls. The company was forced onto older NVIDIA A800 hardware after advanced processors were cut off, slowing its development timelines and constraining its most ambitious capabilities. Jimmy Goodrich of the University of California's Institute on Global Conflict and Cooperation explained the core dependency: 'Chinese security services are dealing with an overload of data. The real value of AI is that they can triage it and find the threats. But their ability to scale that depends on their access to compute.'

This is where the story snaps into acute political relevance. The Trump administration has been systematically unwinding those very controls. In January 2026, the Commerce Department shifted its license review policy for NVIDIA's H200 chip — one of the most advanced available — from a presumption of denial to case-by-case review. The administration explicitly scrapped Biden's broader AI Diffusion Rule last May, calling it a source of 'burdensome regulatory requirements.' As of this week, Senator Elizabeth Warren sent a letter on June 4 formally inviting NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang to testify before the Senate Banking Committee on June 11 about his company's China sales and export control views — a hearing that will now be coloured by the Geedge revelations in ways that could not have been anticipated when the invitation was drafted.

The competing interpretations here are genuine and substantive, not simply partisan noise. The administration and Nvidia argue that the chip controls were counterproductive: they ceded market ground to Chinese domestic producers like Huawei, enriched competitors, and failed to stop China from developing competitive AI models anyway. DeepSeek's emergence is the headline exhibit for this view. From this perspective, a managed commercial relationship — with volume caps, know-your-customer rules, and revenue sharing — is both more economically rational and more strategically honest about what controls can actually achieve.

The counter-argument, pressed by former National Security Adviser Jake Sullivan and now amplified by the Geedge documents, is that this framing confuses frontier AI model development with compute-intensive surveillance applications. The Vanderbilt report is explicit: Geedge does not need to train a competitive frontier model. It needs brute-force GPU capacity to process millions of citizens' behavioral streams in near-real-time. That is exactly the application that chip controls were designed to constrain — and exactly where the controls appear to have worked. The policy loosening, on this reading, is not a reasonable strategic trade-off. It is handing the precise tool the surveillance apparatus needs, at the moment the documents confirm the tool is the binding constraint.

There is also a third dimension that the policy debate largely ignores: the export of the surveillance infrastructure itself. No existing international legal framework clearly prohibits the sale of predictive political surveillance AI to authoritarian states. Geedge is not on any entity list for its surveillance exports to Myanmar or Ethiopia. The Vanderbilt report calls this the real regulatory gap — the absence of any binding multilateral norm against exporting pre-crime AI as a human rights violation.

Watch for three things in the coming weeks. First, Jensen Huang's June 11 Senate testimony will be the most politically charged hearing on chip exports since the Biden controls were first imposed, now freighted with the Geedge context. Second, watch whether the BIS issues any new entity list additions specifically targeting Geedge or MESA Lab — that would be a direct policy response and would signal which faction within the administration is winning the internal argument. Third, watch whether the EU's newly announced tech sovereignty package, released June 3, includes any provisions on surveillance AI export controls — Brussels has been the more aggressive actor on AI governance, and this story gives it a concrete human rights hook on which to hang binding rules.
deep_dive.why_today: Senator Elizabeth Warren's June 4 letter formally inviting Jensen Huang to testify on chip exports to China on June 11 lands directly atop the week's Geedge Networks revelations, fusing the surveillance AI story with an imminent, high-stakes congressional confrontation over the Trump administration's chip policy reversals.

sources.name: Vanderbilt Institute of National Security Report

pattern_watch.title: The Hormuz Squeeze is Funding AI's Power Crisis
pattern_watch.subtitle: Energy scarcity in the Gulf is spiking grid capacity prices 10x over, making transformer shortages a feature, not a bug—and accelerating the shift to captive power models that lock in hyperscaler dominance.
pattern_watch.content: The Strait of Hormuz closure at 5% traffic is a geopolitical fait accompli masquerading as temporary. Trump's ceasefire framework is not holding the strait open—it's holding a diplomatic fiction while Iran maintains a shadow blockade and Hezbollah rejects the latest deal entirely. This is not ending in 60 days. Meanwhile, that same energy crisis is making PJM capacity market prices hit $329/MW—ten times the $29/MW from two years ago—and transformer lead times have exploded to 160+ weeks. Hyperscalers cannot wait for grid infrastructure to catch up. Meta's Columbus campus running on captive natural gas, Microsoft's multi-GW wind PPAs in Europe, and the broader 'Bring Your Own Power' model are not optimization tactics—they are the only path forward when the grid is physically unavailable. This creates a durable structural advantage: whoever locked in power early (Meta, Microsoft, Google via their Salton Sea geothermal deals) will own the 30-50% of announced 2026 capacity that gets delayed or cancelled. Anthropic and SpaceX are filing IPOs at $965B and $2T valuations respectively on the assumption that AI capex scales to $650B+ in 2026. It does not. The grid says no. The winners are already set.

pattern_watch.title: Anthropic's IPO Valuation is a Hostage to the Mythos Vulnerability Cascade
pattern_watch.subtitle: Trump's voluntary AI security EO buys time for labs but weaponizes NSA benchmarking into a de facto regulatory moat—and Anthropic's $47B revenue claim crumbles if Claude's 10,000-per-month vulnerability rate becomes public.
pattern_watch.content: Anthropic's confidential S-1 at $965B valuation sits atop a buried landmine: the Mythos Preview found over 10,000 critical-severity vulnerabilities in a single month, including a wolfSSL flaw affecting 5 billion devices, and Palo Alto Networks has essentially said that three weeks of AI-assisted hacking now equals a year of manual pentesting. That window before attackers access equivalent capability is, Palo Alto stated bluntly, 'accelerated significantly.' Trump's June 2 executive order is being celebrated as innovation-friendly—it is, intentionally, a regulatory hedge. The voluntary 30-day preview to NSA and CISA, combined with NSA's new mandate to build a classified benchmarking process, creates plausible deniability for Anthropic and others: they can claim they cooperated, they can claim the government has classified sight lines into the vulnerability landscape, and yet nothing public ever materializes. This is regulatory capture disguised as light touch. But the real risk to Anthropic's $965B valuation is that a single high-profile exploit chains back to Claude, or that auditors in the S-1 review process demand disclosure of the vulnerability density (10,000+ per month is **not** a normal engineering metric). Daniela Amodei is 'deflecting doubts about AI's ROI' publicly—the IPO is priced on faith that these models have defensible competitive moats. They do not. They have liability landmines.

pattern_watch.title: AI Job Cuts Are the Canary in a Deflationary White-Collar Collapse
pattern_watch.subtitle: 38,579 AI-attributed job cuts in May is the highest monthly total ever recorded, but the Fed is holding rates steady because Hormuz energy inflation (3.8% YoY) masks the real signal: wage destruction at the white-collar layer.
pattern_watch.content: The May jobs report landing this morning is the headline; the actual story is the Challenger, Gray & Christmas data from May: 38,579 job cuts attributed to AI adoption—the highest monthly total since they began tracking in 2023. Yahoo Finance framed this as 'the question for investors: does this remain a tech restructuring story, or does it bleed into broader white-collar labor markets?' It has already bled. The Fed is holding rates at 3.5-3.75% because headline CPI is 3.8% YoY, inflated by Hormuz-driven energy costs. But look underneath: average hourly earnings rose 3.6% in April, and real wage growth for hourly workers is deeply negative. The Fed sees energy shock and holds. The labor market sees AI-driven displacement at scale and... nothing happens, because the energy story is loud enough to drown it out. Anthropic, SpaceX, and Cognition (which just raised a $1B+ Series D at $26B valuation) are all going public on the premise that AI is a productivity multiplier that creates new job categories. It is not. It is a deflationary impulse wrapped in growth narrative. When the Hormuz ceasefire breaks (and it will), energy inflation will fade, headline CPI will drop, the Fed will cut rates, and a labor market already hollowed out by AI displacement will face demand destruction simultaneously. The three-week outflow from crypto funds ($4B+) and Bitcoin's crash to $61,300 are not causally linked to labor data, but they are symptomatic of the same unease: assets are priced for a world where energy scarcity is the only constraint. They are not priced for a world where AI has already begun collapsing white-collar demand.

pattern_watch.title: China's Renminbi Pivot via Hormuz Crisis Is the Real Geopolitical Win
pattern_watch.subtitle: As ships pay tolls to Iran in crypto and renminbi to transit Hormuz, Beijing's CIPS system surges—and the dollar's petrocurrency monopoly develops its first genuine structural threat since Bretton Woods.
pattern_watch.content: The ECB's June 2026 report on the international role of the euro noted in passing that settlement activity on China's CIPS cross-border payment system 'surged around the outbreak of the Iran war,' with some ships making payments in renminbi or crypto to transit the Strait of Hormuz in March-April 2026. This is not a footnote. This is the structural bifurcation of global finance playing out in real time. For 80 years, control of the Strait of Hormuz meant control of the dollar's petrocurrency premium—you paid oil prices in dollars, you settled in dollars, you held reserves in dollars. Iran's blockade and the Trump administration's inaction on Hormuz de-mining (it is 'underway' but 'not yet implemented' after six weeks) has created an economic void. China filled it. Ships that cannot rely on dollar-based insurance, dollar-based letters of credit, or dollar-based settlement can use renminbi and CIPS. The ECB specifically noted that cross-border settlement on CIPS surged—not crypto, not barter, but China's digital payment infrastructure. Separately, China's demand for high-end semiconductor imports surged in Q1 2026 driven by rising AI investment, and Beijing's 'anti-involution' subsidies are flooding export markets with excess auto capacity (60.6% YoY surge in Q1 exports). This is not coincidental: China is simultaneously building AI capacity, flooding export markets to earn hard currency, and establishing an alternative settlement layer for global trade that bypasses the dollar. Anthropic is filing a $965B IPO priced in dollars. Cognition raised $1B+ in dollars. Every hyperscaler is buying transformers and building data centers priced in dollars. But if the dollar's petrocurrency premium erodes because Hormuz remains closed and renminbi-based settlement becomes normalized, the real cost of that AI infrastructure capex just rose 20-30% relative to Chinese competitors who are pricing in renminbi and accessing cheaper capital through Beijing's state machinery. The geopolitical winner of the Hormuz crisis is not whoever reopens the strait—it is whoever stops needing it.

signal_scan.connection: Directly engages with the SpaceX S-1 story; his ongoing AI bubble skepticism (short Nvidia/Palantir thesis) is relevant to Anthropic's $965B IPO valuation and AI market exuberance.

signal_scan.name: Ben Thompson
signal_scan.latest_title: An Interview with Ben Thompson at the MoffettNathanson Media, Internet & Communications Conference
signal_scan.summary: Published June 1 and active through June 4; covered the implications of the compute shortage on Aggregation Theory and consumer AI, SpaceX hype and the 'Elon Bargain,' Nvidia and the Neoclouds, and data center discontent. Also published 'The Google Capital Company' analyzing Google's AI capex strategy and equity issuance.
signal_scan.connection: Directly covers the SpaceX S-1, AI data center buildout dynamics, and the capital structures of hyperscalers — all core to today's top stories on IPOs, grid crisis, and AI infrastructure.

signal_scan.name: Paul Graham

signal_scan.connection: Marginal relevance; his ongoing commentary on founders and AI-era startups has background relevance to the labor/AI displacement story and the startup ecosystem around frontier AI.

signal_scan.name: Benedict Evans
signal_scan.summary: Updated his semi-annual macro tech presentation 'AI eats the world' in May 2026; also recently gave a podcast interview with Lenny Rachitsky (June 1) where he framed AI as a 1997-internet-scale shift — significant but with most applications still unbuilt. Posted on Threads that the lack of a good theory for why LLMs work is not proof they have no limits.
signal_scan.connection: His 'AI eats the world' presentation and '1997 moment' framing directly contextualize the Anthropic IPO, frontier AI governance debates, and the data center buildout cycle.

signal_scan.name: Lenny Rachitsky
signal_scan.latest_title: A rational conversation on where AI is actually going with Benedict Evans
signal_scan.summary: Promoted and shared key takeaways from his June 1 podcast with Benedict Evans, summarizing Evans' view that 'we're in 1997 for AI' — a big-deal shift where most of what will be built hasn't been built yet. Active on X promoting the episode.
signal_scan.connection: Directly connects to Anthropic IPO, frontier AI governance, and the broader question of where AI is in its adoption curve — relevant to labor, applied tech, and AI regulation topics.

signal_scan.name: Andrej Karpathy
signal_scan.summary: Announced on May 19 that he joined Anthropic's pretraining team to lead a group using Claude to accelerate pretraining research — the post drew ~3 million views in an hour. He joins as Anthropic files a confidential IPO at a ~$1T valuation.
signal_scan.connection: His Anthropic hire is directly tied to today's top story on the Anthropic IPO; his 'autoresearch' and 'Karpathy Loop' work on AI-accelerated pretraining connects to frontier tech and AI capability advancement.
